file_name: Maheswari et al. - 2024 - Optimized attention-induced multihead convolutional neural network with efficientnetv2-fostered mela.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an Optimized Attention-Induced Multihead Convolutional Neural Network with EfficientNetV2 (AIMCNN-ENetV2) for melanoma classification using dermoscopic images. It employs deep learning for feature extraction and classification, optimized by a Boosted Chimp Optimization Algorithm (BCOA), to distinguish between acral melanoma and benign nevus.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and presents a novel proposed method (AIMCNN-ENetV2-MC) for melanoma classification. It includes original experimental outcomes, performance metrics (e.g., 98.75% accuracy), and comparisons with existing models, which are characteristic of original research rather than a review.

title: Optimized attention‑induced multihead convolutional neural network with efficientnetv2‑fostered melanoma classification using dermoscopic images
brief_summary: This study proposes an Optimized Attention-Induced Multihead Convolutional Neural Network with EfficientNetV2 (AIMCNN-ENetV2-MC) for classifying acral melanoma and benign nevus using dermoscopic images. The methodology employs an Adaptive Distorted Gaussian Matched Filter for noise removal and utilizes the Boosted Chimp Optimization Algorithm (BCOA) to optimize the classifier parameters. The model was implemented using Python and evaluated against existing CNN models such as CNN-AlexNet-MC, RCNN-DenseNet201-MC, and CNN-VGG16-MC. The proposed approach achieved an outstanding overall accuracy of 98.75% with a reduced computation time of 98 seconds. These results demonstrate that the AIMCNN-ENetV2-MC method offers superior performance and efficiency for automated melanoma diagnosis compared to state-of-the-art frameworks.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary malignant class studied in the classification task. The dataset contained 350 images of AM patients from the Yonsei University Health System dataset. The model achieved 96% accuracy for this class on the Yonsei dataset and 98.75% overall accuracy on the ISIC dataset.

Primary benign class studied in the classification task. The dataset contained 374 images of benign nevi from the Yonsei University Health System dataset. The model achieved 98% accuracy for this class on the Yonsei dataset.
Mentioned in the context of accuracy gains on the ISIC database compared to existing models, but not a primary target class for the proposed model's training or evaluation.

all_task_types.details: The model classifies dermoscopic images into two discrete diagnostic categories: acral melanoma (malignant) and benign nevus (benign). The study also evaluates performance on a multi-class ISIC dataset including actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, dermatofibroma, pigmented benign keratosis, seborrheic keratosis, squamous cell carcinoma, and vascular lesion, but the primary proposed method focuses on binary classification of melanoma vs. nevus.

datasets_used.name: Yonsei University Health System dataset
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopic image dataset gathered by Severance Hospital in Seoul, South Korea, used for the primary classification task of acral melanoma and benign nevus.

use.training: Used for training the AIMCNN-ENetV2-MC model (70% of images).
use.testing: Used for testing algorithm performance on unseen photos (10% of images).

datasets_used.name: ISIC database
datasets_used.description: A dataset compiled by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) containing malignant and benign oncological illnesses, used for comparative evaluation against existing models.

use.testing: Used for external comparison and evaluation of the proposed model's performance against state-of-the-art frameworks.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the proposed model and existing models were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, F1-score, computation time) without statistical significance testing.

Yonsei University Health System dataset.description: Primary dataset for binary classification of Acral Melanoma (AM) and Benign Nevus (BN). Evaluated against CNN-AlexNet-MC, RCNN-DenseNet201-MC, and CNN-VGG16-MC.
Yonsei University Health System dataset.notes: The proposed method achieved 20.64%, 21%, and 22.5% greater accuracy for acral melanoma analysis compared to the baselines. It achieved 31.4%, 23.79%, and 24.75% greater accuracy for benign nevus. It attained 15.20%, 20.21%, and 10.22% higher AUC than existing models.

ISIC database.description: External evaluation dataset containing 9 classes (Melanoma, Nevus, Actinic keratosis, Basal cell carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, Pigmented benign keratosis, Seborrheic keratosis, Squamous cell carcinoma, Vascular lesion). Evaluated against CNN-CO-MC, DCNN-SLC-MC, and VGG-16-BSL-MC.

CNN-CO-MC.accuracy_gain_vs_baseline: 22.54% (Actinic keratosis), 20.46% (Basal cell carcinoma), 28.84% (Dermatofibroma), 27.46% (Melanoma), 28.44% (Pigmented benign keratosis), 24.45% (Seborrheic keratosis), 26.47% (Squamous cell carcinoma), 25.42% (Vascular lesion)

DCNN-SLC-MC.accuracy_gain_vs_baseline: 20% (Actinic keratosis), 28.79% (Basal cell carcinoma), 26.59% (Dermatofibroma), 25.65% (Melanoma), 26.79% (Pigmented benign keratosis), 25.72% (Seborrheic keratosis), 25.79% (Squamous cell carcinoma), 27.31% (Vascular lesion)

VGG-16-BSL-MC.accuracy_gain_vs_baseline: 23.5% (Actinic keratosis), 27.75% (Basal cell carcinoma), 27.78% (Dermatofibroma), 24.73% (Melanoma), 22.75% (Pigmented benign keratosis), 26.75% (Seborrheic keratosis), 27.75% (Squamous cell carcinoma), 24.65% (Vascular lesion)

ISIC database.notes: The proposed method achieved an outstanding overall accuracy of 98.75% and a computation time of 98 seconds. It attained 9.78%, 6.52%, and 7.65% lower computation time compared with CNN-CO-MC, DCNN-SLC-MC, and VGG-16-BSL-MC respectively.

architecture_used.main_model: AIMCNN-ENetV2-MC (Optimized Attention-Induced Multihead Convolutional Neural Network with EfficientNetV2-fostered Melanoma Classification)
architecture_used.pretraining: EfficientNetV2 pre-trained weights integrated into the AIMCNN architecture; transfer learning utilized from large-scale datasets.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (dermoscopic images from Yonsei University Health System and ISIC datasets).

architecture_details.backbone: EfficientNetV2 (ENetV2) replacing the convolutional layer of the base AIMCNN model.
architecture_details.feature_extraction: AIMCNN with multi-head attention mechanism; extracts color histogram, Hu Moments, and Haralick Texture features (contrast, correlation, energy, homogeneity, skewness, variance, standard deviation, entropy).
architecture_details.layers: Seven-layered AIMCNN structure. Includes four convolutional layers, connected layers, and a dense layer. The original AIMCNN convolutional layer (1000 neurons) was swapped for EfficientNetV2. A new fully connected layer with 256 neurons was added after the EfficientNetV2 block.
architecture_details.heads: Multi-head attention mechanism within the AIMCNN.
architecture_details.optimization_algorithm: Boosted Chimp Optimization Algorithm (BCOA) used to optimize weight parameters (Ib) and tune bias parameters of the AIMCNN-ENetV2 classifier.

training_details.hardware: PC with Intel Core i5, 2.50GHz CPU, 8GB RAM, Windows 7
training_details.dataset: Yonsei University Health System dataset (724 images: 350 Acral Melanoma, 374 Benign Nevus)
training_details.classes: Binary classification: Acral Melanoma vs. Benign Nevus

classification_or_output_head.output: Binary classification (Acral Melanoma or Benign Nevus)
classification_or_output_head.neurons: 256 neurons in the final fully connected layer before Softmax

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: AIMCNN-ENetV2-MC (Optimized Attention-Induced Multihead Convolutional Neural Network with EfficientNetV2-fostered Melanoma Classification)
preprocessing_techniques: Adaptive Distorted Gaussian Matched Filter (ADGMF) for noise removal and artifact removal (dark corner artifact frames, ruler markings, and hairs)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to address overfitting in the network. Specific geometric or color transform parameters (e.g., rotation angles, flip probabilities) were not reported in the text.

conclusions: The proposed Optimized Attention-Induced Multihead Convolutional Neural Network with EfficientNetV2 (AIMCNN-ENetV2-MC) effectively classifies acral melanoma and benign nevus using dermoscopic images.
The integration of EfficientNetV2 into the AIMCNN architecture, optimized by the Boosted Chimp Optimization Algorithm (BCOA), enhances feature extraction and classification accuracy.
The model achieved an overall accuracy of 98.75% on the ISIC database and 96% accuracy for acral melanoma and 98% for benign nevus on the Yonsei University Health System dataset.

The proposed method demonstrated superior performance compared to existing models (CNN-AlexNet-MC, RCNN-DenseNet201-MC, CNN-VGG16-MC) with significant gains in accuracy (e.g., 20.64% greater for acral melanoma on Yonsei dataset) and F1-scores.

The AIMCNN-ENetV2-MC method reduced computation time significantly (69 seconds on Yonsei dataset, 98 seconds on ISIC dataset) compared to baseline models, making it suitable for real-time clinical applications.
The use of Adaptive Distorted Gaussian Matched Filter (ADGMF) for noise removal and data augmentation helped address challenges related to limited data and artifacts in dermoscopic images.
The study acknowledges that manual diagnosis is time-consuming and prone to human error, highlighting the need for automated computer-aided diagnostics for early melanoma detection.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (acral melanoma vs. benign nevus) on a small, geographically specific dataset (Yonsei University Health System, South Korea), which limits generalizability to other melanoma subtypes or diverse populations.

The external evaluation on the ISIC database was performed using a pre-trained model without retraining or fine-tuning on the ISIC data, serving only as a benchmark comparison rather than a true test of the proposed model's adaptability to new data distributions.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was reported to validate the significance of performance differences between the proposed model and baselines.
The dataset size is small (724 images total), raising potential concerns about overfitting despite the use of data augmentation and dropout regularization.
The hardware environment for evaluation (Intel Core i5, 8GB RAM) is modest, which may not reflect performance on clinical-grade or mobile deployment hardware.
The paper does not report inter-observer variability or ground truth verification processes for the dataset labels, which is a critical factor in dermatological AI studies.
